mission.title: Install and Configure Armada on Kubernetes
mission.description: Armada is a multi-cluster batch queuing system designed for high-throughput workloads on Kubernetes. Installing Armada allows you to efficiently manage and schedule jobs across multiple Kubernetes clusters, making it ideal for large-scale batch processing needs.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Armada Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add armada https://charts.armadaproject.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Armada using Helm
steps.description:
Install Armada in a new namespace called 'armada' with the specified version:
```bash
helm install armada armada/armada --namespace armada --create-namespace --version v0.20.34
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all pods are running in the 'armada' namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace armada
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal resource usage, edit the Armada deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment armada --namespace armada
``` 
In the editor, add the following under the container spec:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "1000m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up Prometheus monitoring
steps.description:
To enable monitoring with Prometheus, you can install the Prometheus operator and configure it to scrape metrics from Armada. First, install the Prometheus operator:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/prometheus-operator/prometheus-operator/main/bundle.yaml
```
Then, create a ServiceMonitor for Armada:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: armada-monitor
  namespace: armada
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: armada
  endpoints:
  - port: metrics
    interval: 30s
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Armada will show all pods running in the 'armada' namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace armada`. Additionally, you will have configured resource limits and set up Prometheus monitoring for your Armada instance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add armada https://charts.armadaproject.io
helm repo update
helm install armada armada/armada --namespace armada --create-namespace --version v0.20.34

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Armada release from the 'armada' namespace using Helm to remove the deployment and associated resources:
```bash
helm uninstall armada --namespace armada
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Armada:
```bash
kubectl delete crd armadajobs.armada.k8s.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace armada
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'armada' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace armada
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Armada deployment:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace armada -o yaml > armada-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Armada release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade armada armada/armada --namespace armada --version v0.20.35
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all pods are running and the upgrade was successful:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace armada
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in the 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for the failing pod:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace armada
```
Investigate the logs for errors and adjust the configuration or resource limits as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: ServiceMonitor not scraping metrics
troubleshooting.description:
If Prometheus is not scraping metrics from Armada, verify the ServiceMonitor configuration:
```bash
kubectl get servicemonitor armada-monitor --namespace armada -o yaml
```
Ensure that the selector matches the labels of the Armada service.

troubleshooting.title: Deployment not available
troubleshooting.description:
If the Armada deployment is not available, check the deployment status:
```bash
kubectl get deployment armada --namespace armada
```
If the replicas are not available, describe the deployment for more details:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment armada --namespace armada
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being terminated with 'OOMKilled', check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace armada
```
Consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment configuration.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
